# A deep learning framework for quantitative spatial analysis of the tumor microenvironment

**Ziqi Kang^1^, Matias Aiskovich^1^, Fernando Perez^1^, Angela Szabo^1^,
Lilian van Wagensveld^5,\ 6,\ 7^, Hugo Horlings^7^,** **Esa
Pitkänen^2,\ 3,\ 4^, Anniina Färkkilä^1,\ 2,\ 3^**

Finland

Sciences, University of Helsinki, Finland

^3^ iCAN Digital Precision Cancer Medicine Flagship, Finland

^6^ GROW, School for Oncology and Developmental Biology, Maastricht
University, 6229 ER Maastricht, the Netherlands

^7^ Division of Molecular Pathology, The Netherlands Cancer Institute,
1066 CX Amsterdam, The Netherlands

# A deep learning framework for quantitative spatial analysis of the tumor microenvironment

**Ziqi Kang^1^, Matias Aiskovich^1^, Fernando Perez^1^, Angela Szabo^1^,
Lilian van Wagensveld^5,\ 6,\ 7^, Hugo Horlings^7^,** **Esa
Pitkänen^2,\ 3,\ 4^, Anniina Färkkilä^1,\ 2,\ 3^**

^1^ Research Program in Systems Oncology, University of Helsinki,
Finland

^2^ Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland, Helsinki Institute of Life
Sciences, University of Helsinki, Finland

^3^ iCAN Digital Precision Cancer Medicine Flagship, Finland

^4^ Applied Tumor Genomics Research Program, University of Helsinki,
Finland

^5^ Department of Research and Development, Netherlands Comprehensive
Cancer Organization (IKNL), 3511 DT Utrecht, the Netherlands

^6^ GROW, School for Oncology and Developmental Biology, Maastricht
University, 6229 ER Maastricht, the Netherlands

^7^ Division of Molecular Pathology, The Netherlands Cancer Institute,
1066 CX Amsterdam, The Netherlands

Current multiplexed immunofluorescence (mIF) image analysis relies on
quantitative description of single-cell objects, however, cell
segmentation is becoming a bottleneck for tissue architecture
description since it fails in dense regions and causes loss of
information in extracellular areas. There is a need to define novel
representations of complex tissue architecture, one possible solution is

ls in dense regions and causes loss of
information in extracellular areas. There is a need to define novel
representations of complex tissue architecture, one possible solution is
to extract biologically meaningful multi-scale features directly from
images without cell segmentation.

We present TMEncoder, a deep learning framework based on the variational
autoencoder (VAE) capable of extracting features of tumor
microenvironments based on multiplex-stained tumor tissue images without
expert-annotated labels. Based on the scale of input datasets, TMEncoder
could unsupervised learn corresponding features from local tissue
archetypes to tumor subtypes. TMEncoder incorporates multiple advanced
techniques including patch generation, intermediate fusion strategy, and
a novel pipeline for quantitative analysis of tumor microenvironment
elements. Therefore, our framework can process multi-channel images.

We applied TMEncoder to an in-house 33-plex tissue microarray (TMA)
dataset collected from a cohort of 241 high-grade serous ovarian cancer
(HGSOC) patients and successfully recovered biological features related
to tumor molecular subtypes. Then, we are planning to expand the
analysis to two external datasets of human cancer tissue images to
discover the tumor microenvironment features: a 56-plex TMA dataset
including 140 images from 35 advanced-stage colorectal cancer (CRC)

ls in dense regions and causes loss of
information in extracellular areas. There is a need to define novel
representations of complex tissue architecture, one possible solution is
to extract biologically meaningful multi-scale features directly from

We present TMEncoder, a deep learning framework based on the variational
autoencoder (VAE) capable of extracting features of tumor
microenvironments based on multiplex-stained tumor tissue images without
expert-annotated labels. Based on the scale of input datasets, TMEncoder
could unsupervised learn corresponding features from local tissue
archetypes to tumor subtypes. TMEncoder incorporates multiple advanced
techniques including patch generation, intermediate fusion strategy, and
a novel pipeline for quantitative analysis of tumor microenvironment
elements. Therefore, our framework can process multi-channel images.

We applied TMEncoder to an in-house 33-plex tissue microarray (TMA)
dataset collected from a cohort of 241 high-grade serous ovarian cancer
(HGSOC) patients and successfully recovered biological features related
to tumor molecular subtypes. Then, we are planning to expand the
analysis to two external datasets of human cancer tissue images to
discover the tumor microenvironment features: a 56-plex TMA dataset
including 140 images from 35 advanced-stage colorectal cancer (CRC)

l datasets of human cancer tissue images to
discover the tumor microenvironment features: a 56-plex TMA dataset
including 140 images from 35 advanced-stage colorectal cancer (CRC)
patients; and a 35-plex imaging mass cytometry (IMC) dataset from 416
lung adenocarcinoma patients.

In summary, the novel image analysis strategy enabled by TMEncoder shows
promise to strengthen the understanding of tumor biology and re-visit
the hidden information behind multiplexed images. Combining such
information with classical single-cell-based analysis leads to an
improved understanding of the tumor microenvironment and novel precision
therapies.

(273 words)

l datasets of human cancer tissue images to
discover the tumor microenvironment features: a 56-plex TMA dataset
including 140 images from 35 advanced-stage colorectal cancer (CRC)
patients; and a 35-plex imaging mass cytometry (IMC) dataset from 416
lung adenocarcinoma patients.

In summary, the novel image analysis strategy enabled by TMEncoder shows
promise to strengthen the understanding of tumor biology and re-visit
the hidden information behind multiplexed images. Combining such
information with classical single-cell-based analysis leads to an
improved understanding of the tumor microenvironment and novel precision
